The next day, medical residents presented their cases during the ICU's morning report in shorthand, at auctioneer-like speed. There were many patients to get through.

Over the course of a day, the doctors treated one of their own residents and the mother of a beloved colleague. They treated pregnant women, some of whom wouldn't be conscious when their babies were delivered to increase their odds of surviving to raise their children.

Some of the ICU patients are in their 80s; others in their 30s. Some have asthma or diabetes, but others seemed to have no risk factors at all.
The message from the overwhelmed staff remains the same: "Stay home. Protect yourself."

There was every reason to believe that movement restrictions imposed to keep the coronavirus from spreading would exacerbate abuse, experts said. http://nyti.ms/3b0mTwo

But governments largely failed to prepare for the way new public health measures would create opportunities for abusers to terrorize their victims.
Now they're scrambling. And as with the response to the virus itself, delays mean that irreparable harm may have already occurred.

In Spain, calls to the domestic violence emergency number went up in the first 2 weeks of lockdown.
In France, police reported a roughly 30% nationwide spike in domestic violence.
In China, one NGO said the call surge began when officials locked down cities in the epicenter.

Judith Lewis Herman, a renowned trauma expert at Harvard, found the methods abusers use to control their partners and children bear "an uncanny resemblance" to those kidnappers use to control hostages and repressive regimes use to break the will of political prisoners.

In Europe, several countries followed the same grim path:
First, governments impose lockdowns without enough planning for abuse victims.
Days later, distress calls spike, setting off public outcry.
Only then do officials improvise solutions, like turning hotels into shelters.

Eventually, lockdowns will end. But as the confinement drags on, the danger is likely to intensify. Studies show abusers are more likely to murder their partners and others in the wake of personal crises, including lost jobs.

But when Dr. Mayorquin left work on a Friday expecting to pick up her 2 daughters for the weekend, her soon-to-be ex-husband presented her with a court order granting him sole temporary custody of their children.

Dr. Mayorquin had intended to spend the weekend celebrating her younger daughter’s 8th birthday. Instead, she spent it frantically assembling 50 pages of paperwork to try to reverse the court order. https://nyti.ms/34iTgnG

For health care and other essential workers, the custody battles are infused with heightened controversy. Some of those workers insist they are taking precautions to keep their children safe and shouldn’t be punished for doing crucial services.
But as their counterparts sometimes see it, the jobs pose too great a risk to other members of their families https://nyti.ms/34iTgnG

The federal response was chaotic and dysfunctional from the earliest days and serious problems with testing made it far harder to gauge the scope of the outbreak. As a result, city and state officials often had to make decisions with little assistance from the federal government.
But from the start, even after the first confirmed case was announced on March 1, de Blasio and Cuomo worried as much about panic as they did about the virus.

Each day in early March brought new action: restrictions on gatherings, reduced bar occupancy. But the biggest battle was closing the school system. It wasn't until March 15, after alarmed health officials forecasted a chilling death toll, that de Blasio was finally persuaded.
The mayor said, 2 days later, that something similar to a shelter-in-place order would soon need to be imposed for New Yorkers. This time, Cuomo resisted. He favored a more gradual shutdown.

The governor, the mayor and top aides emphasized they had no misgivings about their initial handling of the response.
"I think New York was early," Cuomo said at a news conference on Wednesday. "I think the actions we took were more dramatic than most."

The U.S. isn't doing nearly enough coronavirus testing to safely reopen, new estimates by Harvard University researchers suggest — in fact, it will take 3 times as much testing over the next month.
Here's why — and a look at which states are most behind.
SOMETIMES A FORBIDDEN PERSON EMERGES FROM THE FLOOR AND TURNS ON A RANDOM APPLIANCE. THERE IS NO KNOWN FIX FOR THIS

First, the numbers:
146,000 — that's how many people on average were tested for the coronavirus each day in the U.S. this month, according to the COVID Tracking Project.
500,000 to 700,000 — that's how many daily tests are needed to reopen by mid-May, the Harvard estimates say.

A shortage of test kits and technical flaws in the U.S. significantly delayed widespread coronavirus testing.
This is how testing has increased since the beginning of March — and how far it still needs to go, according to the Harvard estimates. https://nyti.ms/3cnVsNs

A high rate of positive tests suggests that most people with the virus aren't being tested.
About 20% of people tested in the U.S. have been positive — twice the maximum rate recommended by the WHO. https://nyti.ms/3cnVsNs

State by state in the U.S., there's a wide variation in the rate of testing and positive results. But most need to administer more tests to get to the level the researchers suggest — a minimum of about 152 tests per 100,000 people. https://nyti.ms/3cnVsNs

New Jersey currently has the highest positive rate of all states — about half of its 157,000 coronavirus tests have come back positive. That suggests that many of the people in the state who have the coronavirus have not been tested. http://nyti.ms/3cnVsNs
We looked at coronavirus testing across every state in the U.S. See how your state compares — and how close it is to what experts say is needed to safely reopen.

A fire on a submarine may be a mariner’s worst nightmare, but the deadly fire last year aboard a Russian nuclear sub was a threat of another order altogether. @jamesglanz and @nilsenthomas investigated what happened aboard the sub — the Losharik.

The only thing more mysterious than what went wrong aboard the Losharik, a deep-diving military submarine, was what it was doing under 1,000 feet of Arctic water just east of Norway in the first place. https://nyti.ms/3eMhP1g

The Russian sub appeared to be on a mission of the highest sensitivity, and the 14 crewmen who died in the accident included some of the most decorated and experienced officers of the Russian submarine corps. https://nyti.ms/3eMhP1g
Shocking, I tell ya’. Just shocking. https://t.co/VS1Tiu0xVs

A Kremlin spokesman said after the accident that information about the sub “belongs to the category of top-secret data.” But our reporters found clues to Russia’s military ambitions in the deep sea, where miles of fiber-optic cables carry much of the world’s internet traffic.

The Losharik, named for a Russian cartoon character, has an inner hull that is thought to consist of titanium spheres. It could dive more than 10x deeper than manned U.S. subs. The design that made it so special may have also made it more dangerous for the crew.

Read @jamesglanz and @nilsenthomas’s investigation for more on the mystery surrounding the Russian submarine’s Arctic mission and Russia’s strategic interests in the deep sea — in English and Russian. https://nyti.ms/2yy6z7U
